does: This paper investigates how three-layer transformers perform in-context learning on affine recurrence tasks through mechanistic interpretability analysis. The authors train a custom transformer to predict sequences following affine recurrence relations and dissect its internal operations using empirical and theoretical methods. They discover a two-stage prediction mechanism: the initial layer generates a crude estimate of the target sequence through copying mechanisms that repeat or interpolate previous sequence elements, while the second layer refines this prediction using negative similarity heads that identify and correct deviations from the true affine pattern. This mechanistic decomposition reveals how the model transitions from simple pattern-matching to learned sequence extrapolation without weight updates.
connects: This work extends the growing body of mechanistic interpretability research examining transformer internals, building on prior studies that decomposed attention heads and feed-forward layers into interpretable components. It directly addresses the in-context learning puzzle that has motivated recent interpretability work seeking to understand how transformers generalize at inference time despite frozen parameters. The focus on affine recurrences as a tractable ICL task connects to earlier research using synthetic sequence prediction tasks to understand algorithm learning in neural networks. The investigation of copying and similarity-based mechanisms relates to interpretability findings about how transformers exploit learned features through attention patterns, extending these insights to a specific recursive prediction domain.
enables: These mechanistic findings open pathways for predicting and controlling transformer behavior on more complex recursive and algorithmic tasks. Understanding how transformers decompose sequence prediction into initialization and refinement stages could guide architecture designs or training procedures that improve both sample efficiency and interpretability of ICL. The identified copying and similarity head operations provide concrete targets for intervention experiments testing causality of specific components. Results may inform alignment efforts by clarifying which internal mechanisms drive generalization behavior, potentially enabling verification or constraints on how models learn from context. The framework of analyzing task-specific mechanism could extend to other ICL capabilities like few-shot learning and reasoning, building interpretable maps of transformer computation.
